coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effect enhancing the energy transfer efficiency of carbon dots-based molecular beacon probe for ultrasensitive detection of microRNA
coredata.dc:creator: He, Mengyuan
Zheng, Bo
Shang, Ning
Xiao, Yu
Wei, Yuanhang
Hu, Xuebo
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive molecular beacon (MB) probe employing carbon dots (CDs) as energy donor and organic dye and graphene oxide (GO) as synergistic quencher was constructed for microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) determination. The MB probe (CDs-MB-BHQ1) was first fabricated by conjugating CDs on the 5′ end of BHQ1-tagged MB, and the fluorescence of CDs decreased owing to the occurrence of luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET). GO was introduced as a synergistic quencher, thus effectively enhancing the energy transfer efficiency through the interaction between GO and the loop region of MB. In the presence of miRNA-21, it hybridized with loop of MB, thus bringing CDs-MB-BHQ1 away from the surface of GO and increasing the distance between CDs and BHQ1. Owing to the inhibition of LRET process, the fluorescence of CDs at 513 nm (excitation at 490 nm) was recovered. This probe exhibited excellent specificity and could be applied to miRNA-21 determination (0.5–800 pM). Moreover, this proposed probe could monitor the content of miRNA-21 in real human serums, and accuracy were verified by qRT-PCR method (Er ≤ 15.3%) and recovery experiments (recoveries ranged from 88.0% to 105%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular beacon
MicroRNA
Carbon dots
Synergistic quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Attachment of molecular beacon to carbon dots
MiRNA-21 detection in buffer and human serum
Analytical performance of the system towards miRNA-21
Determination of miRNA-21 in human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804117 ), the Youth Fund Project of Xinyang Normal University ( 2022-QN-039 ), and the Nanhu Scholars Program for Young Scholars of Xinyang Normal University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs (Insert: the size distribution of CDs). (B) FT-IR spectra of CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of CDs. (D) Emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of (a) CDs, (b) CDs-MB-BHQ1, (c) CDs-MB-BHQ1 upon the addition of GO, (d) CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO probe with the introduction of miRNA-21. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, MB-BHQ1, CDs-MB-BHQ1.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO probe with the addition of various amounts of miRNA-21 (0, 0.5, 1, 10, 20, 40, 80, 400, 800, 1000, 5000 pM). (B) The relationship between (F−F0)/F0 and miRNA-21 concentration (Insert: calibration curve between (F−F0)/F0 and the logarithm of miRNA-21 concentration within the range of 0.5–800 pM).
Selectivity of the CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO probe toward miRNA-21. The number 1–5 represent SM-miRNA-21, DM-miRNA-21, TM-miRNA-21, miRNA-122, and miRNA-21, respectively.
Schematic illustration of CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO system for miRNA-21 detection.
Comparison of the detection results of three real human serum samples between CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO system and qRT-PCR method.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effect enhancing the energy transfer efficiency of carbon dots-based molecular beacon probe for ultrasensitive detection of microRNA

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrasensitive molecular beacon (MB) probe employing carbon dots (CDs) as energy donor and organic dye and graphene oxide (GO) as synergistic quencher was constructed for microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) determination. The MB probe (CDs-MB-BHQ1) was first fabricated by conjugating CDs on the 5′ end of BHQ1-tagged MB, and the fluorescence of CDs decreased owing to the occurrence of luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET). GO was introduced as a synergistic quencher, thus effectively enhancing the energy transfer efficiency through the interaction between GO and the loop region of MB. In the presence of miRNA-21, it hybridized with loop of MB, thus bringing CDs-MB-BHQ1 away from the surface of GO and increasing the distance between CDs and BHQ1. Owing to the inhibition of LRET process, the fluorescence of CDs at 513 nm (excitation at 490 nm) was recovered. This probe exhibited excellent specificity and could be applied to miRNA-21 determination (0.5–800 pM). Moreover, this proposed probe could monitor the content of miRNA-21 in real human serums, and accuracy were verified by qRT-PCR method (Er ≤ 15.3%) and recovery experiments (recoveries ranged from 88.0% to 105%).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of endogenous non-coding RNA molecules, microRNAs (miRNAs) are usually considered as important participants in the post-transcriptional regulation of gene expression, such as development, proliferation and death of human cells [1–3]. Previous research results have confirmed that the aberrant expression of miRNAs are closely related to multiple diseases [4,5]. MiRNA-21, for instance, is overexpressed in most of tumour cells, such as lung cancer and breast cancer. Therefore, miRNAs have become a new generation of biomarker for disease diagnosis, treatment and prognosis [6,7]. Currently, various techniques have been employed to develop biosensors for the quantification of miRNAs, such as colorimetry [8], fluoremetry [9,10], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [11] and electrochemistry [12,13]. Owing to the low abundance, short size and sequence homology of miRNAs, sensitive and selective miRNA detection methods are still highly desired.

Molecular beacons (MBs), oligonucleotides which exhibit a stem-loop structure under ingenious design, have been used as attractive probes for the detection of different kinds of targets [14,15]. The loop region contains a probe sequence against nucleic acid or other target, and the stem region is consists of two short complementary sequences. MBs have been applied as fluorescent probes by labelling a fluorophore at one end as energy donor and a quencher at the other end as energy acceptor to construct LRET system [16,17]. Thanks to the unique structure of MB, the distance between energy donor and acceptor is reduced to molecular size, i.e., less than 1 nm, which increases the quenching efficiency and ensures detection sensitivity [18]. When MB is hybridized with a mismatched sequence, the overall configuration is thermodynamically unstable, thus ensuring the high selectivity of MB probe.

In recent years, MB probes have been widely used in the detection of miRNAs, in which different kinds of fluorophores including organic dyes [19], quantum dots (QDs) [20,21], noble metal nanomaterials [22], carbon dots [23–25], persistent luminescence nanoparticles (PLNPs) [26], and so on have been used as energy donors. Most of these MB probes are dedicated to developing new energy donors to improve analytical performance, while using organic dyes as energy acceptors. Due to the narrow absorption band and low quenching efficiency of organic dyes [27,28], it is difficult to construct a spectra matched donor/acceptor system. In addition, the detection sensitivity of MB probes for miRNAs detection need to be further improved.

Graphene oxide (GO), an emerging two-dimensional (2D) graphitic carbon nanomaterial, has recently attracted significant attention as nano-quencher of LRET system owing to its wide absorption band and outstanding quenching ability [29,30]. Single-strand DNA (ssDNA) can adsorb onto GO via hydrophobic and π–π stacking interaction, while release from GO when it hybridize with single stranded nucleic acid molecules or react with other targets [31,32]. Hairpin DNA has been evidenced to interact with GO in the same way as ssDNA. Some interesting sensing platforms have been developed on the basis of this principle for the detection of different kind of targets [33–35]. Therefore, GO is an effective energy acceptor of LRET system based on hairpin DNA.

In this work, a MB probe using green emitting carbon dots (CDs) as energy donor and organic dye and GO as synergistic quencher was developed for the determination of miRNA-21 (Scheme 1 ). The loop region of MB was cleverly designed as a complementary sequence of miRNA-21. The MB probe (CDs-MB-BHQ1) was formed by conjugating CDs on the 5‘ end of BHQ1-tagged MB, and the fluorescence of CDs decreased owing to the triggering of LRET process. With the introduction of GO, the fluorescence of CDs is further quenched as a result of the π-π stacking interaction between the loop region of MB and GO. When miRNA-21 exists, it hybridizes with the loop region of MB, resulting in the release of CDs-MB-BHQ1 from GO. Meanwhile, the hairpin structure of MB is opened and the fluorescence of CDs is restored because of the inhibition of LRET process, thus realizing miRNA-21 detection. In our design, the introduction of GO could effectively reduce background signal and improve detection sensitivity. Compared to LRET systems that only using GO as energy acceptor, the dosage of nanomaterials is greatly reduced, as low as micrograms per liter, which effectively circumventing the problem of large size and poor colloidal stability of nanoparticles.

N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride) (EDC) using as crosslinking agent were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Calcium citrate and urea using as raw material of CDs were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The dialysis membrane with Molecular Weight Cut Off (MWCO) of 1000 was purchased from Spectrumlabs (USA). Graphene oxide with sheet diameter of 100–300 nm and single layer thickness of 0.33 ∼ 0.39 nm was provided by Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China) and ultrasonicized for 5 h before use to obtain uniform dispersion. DEPC-treated water, DEPC-treated PBS buffer (20×, pH 7.4), and the nucleotide sequences were supplied by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Their sequences are list in Table S1. The molecular beacon labelled with –NH2 at 5′ end and BHQ1 at 3′ end. All solutions used in this work were prepared using DEPC-treated water. PBS buffer was first diluted 20 times before use and all DNA and miRNA samples were prepared using this processed PBS buffer.

Tecnai G2 F 20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, America) was used for taking TEM images of CDs, GO and CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO composites. Nicolet 5 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) was used to measure the FT-IR spectra of CDs. Hitachi F7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was applied to test the fluorescence spectra. Shimadzu UV mini-1240 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to test the UV–Vis absorbance spectra. P70D20TL-D4 microwave oven (Guangdong Galanz Microwave Life Appliances Manufacturing Co., Ltd., China) was used for the preparation of CDs.

CDs were prepared using microwave method according to previous reports with some modification [36]. Briefly, 3 g of calcium citrate and 3 g of urea were added into 10 mL of deionized water and stirred for 10 min, and then put into microwave oven with the power of 700 W for 5 min. The obtained yellow cluster solid was dissolved with an appropriate amount of deionized water. After removing large particles through centrifuging, the supernatant was collected carefully and dialyzed against ultrapure water for 72 h. CDs (10 mg/mL) was obtained by drying and redispersion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attachment of molecular beacon to carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The amine modified molecular beacons were attached on the surface of CDs with the assistant of EDC and NHS. Briefly, 10 μL of CDs was dispersed in 950 μL MES (10 mM, pH 5.5) buffer and kept ultrasonic for 5 min to ensure uniform dispersion. 10 μL of EDC (10 mg/mL) and 20 μL NHS (10 mg/mL) were added respectively and then incubated at room temperature with slow shaking for 1 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of 10 μM molecular beacon was introduced and lasted overnight at room temperature. The CDs-MB-BHQ1 bioconjugate (0.1 mg/mL) was obtained and stored at 4℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MiRNA-21 detection in buffer and human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the dosage of GO, 10 μL of 0.1 mg/mL CDs-MB-BHQ1 solution, different amounts of GO, and various volumes of PBS buffer were mixed in the centrifuge tube and the total volume were adjusted to 600 μL. After incubating at room temperature for 30 min, fluorescence spectra measurement were carried out. The detection of miRNA-21 was first performed in buffer. Briefly, 35 μL GO (0.1 mg/mL) was first added into CDs-MB-BHQ1 solution and incubated 30 min at room temperature. Thereafter, various amounts of miRNA-21 were added follow by incubation at 37 ℃ for another 1.5 h. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded. To examine the specificity of the CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO toward miRNA-21, another miRNA, e.g. miRNA 122, and base mismatched miRNAs were introduced and detected with the same experimental conditions and procedures. To realize miRNA-21 detection in real human serum, 0.6 μL freshly collected human serum was introduced to CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO system directly, following by a procedure identical to that in buffer.

To realize our design, CDs with bright green light emission were first prepared using microwave method and characterized. As can be seen from Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared CDs are nearly spherical particle, and the diameter is in the range of 1.5–2.7 nm (insert of Fig. 1A). FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1B) shows typical peaks of O-H stretching vibration (3500–2500 cm−1), O-H in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibration (1577 cm−1 and 755 cm−1), C = O and C-O stretching vibration (1703 cm−1 and 1365 cm−1), which indicate the existence of carboxyl on the surface of CDs. As illustrated in the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1C), the maximum excitation wavelength of CDs is centered at 490 nm. Under the excitation of this wavelength, CDs emit bright green fluorescence located at 513 nm. Interestingly, excitation-independent fluorescence emission behaviour can be observed in Fig. 1D. When excited with light of different wavelengths (380–490 nm), the maximum emission wavelength of CDs remains unchanged, indicating that CDs have uniform size distribution and surface state. The quantum yield (QY) of this green emitting CDs was determined by using of fluorescein in 0.1 M NaOH solution (QY 95% at 496 nm excitation) as a reference dye. The QY was calculated to be 10.6 %. To evaluate the practical application ability of the green emitting CDs, the photostability was examined by monitoring its fluorescence emission for a long time. The change of fluorescence intensity at 513 nm is negligible (＜3%) when the light source is continuously irradiated for 30 min (Fig. S1). All of above results show that CDs have great application potential in bio-related applications.

The amine modified MB-BHQ1 is conjugated to CDs using EDC and NHS as biological crosslinking agent. After conjugating with MB-BHQ1, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decrease significantly (curve a and b in Fig. 2 A). The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, MB-BHQ1, and CDs-MB-BHQ1 are shown in Fig. 2B. The absorption peak at 260 nm on the curve of the MB-BHQ1 is belong to the absorption of ssDNA. CDs shows maximum absorbance located at 490 nm. Compared to CDs and MB-BHQ1, the absorbance of CDs-MB-BHQ1 complex at 260 nm significantly increases, while the absorbance at 490 nm keeps almost unchanged. All of these indicate that MB-BHQ1 is conjugated on the surface of CDs successfully and BHQ1 can quench the fluorescence of CDs to a certain degree. There was no obvious change in fluorescence intensity of CDs-MB-BHQ1 after storage at 4℃ for seven days (Fig. S2), ensuring the practical application ability of this developed method.

The feasibility of CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO sensing system was first verified. The fluorescence intensity of CDs is firstly quenched by BHQ1 to some extent after bioconjugation step. When GO is introduced into CDs-MB-BHQ1, the fluorescence intensity of CDs further decreases, and the quenching efficiency increases (curve c in Fig. 2A). To intuitively proof the successful interaction between GO and CDs-MB-BHQ1, TEM images of GO and CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO complex were measured respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, CDs can be clearly observed on the surface of GO, confirming the successful formation of CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO complex. Then a certain amount of miRNA-21 was introduced into CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO system and the fluorescence intensity of CDs remarkably restore (curve d in Fig. 2A), demonstrating the feasibility of this design.

The dosage of GO were then optimized. Fluorescence intensity of CDs-MB-BHQ1 is gradually quenched with the increase of GO concentration and then reach a plateau (Fig. S4). There was a nearly 25% decrease in fluorescence emission of the CDs-MB-BHQ1 when the amount of GO was 3.5 μg/mL, which was chosen for subsequent experiments. It is worth mentioning that the concentration of GO used in this strategy is 1–2 order of magnitude lower than that of other reported GO sensors [33,34], which effectively reduce the influence of nanomaterials on the stability of the detection system, thus improving the reproducibility of the test results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the system towards miRNA-21
ce:section.ce:para: MiRNA-21 determination was first carried out in PBS buffer, which was implemented by adding different concentrations of miRNA-21 into CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO system. A remarkable fluorescence recovery related to miRNA-21 concentration can be observed (Fig. 3 A). After miRNA-21 concentration reaching 800 pM, the fluorescence intensity of the system remains basically unchanged (Fig. 3B). A calibration curve between fluorescence recovery degree and the logarithm of miRNA-21 concentration is obtained from 0.5 pM to 800 pM under the calibration curve equation (F − F0)/F0 = 0.09016lgcmiRNA-21 + 0.1429 (insert of Fig. 3B). To verify the improvement effect of GO on detection performance, CDs-MB-BHQ1 probe was applied to determine miRNA-21 directly without GO follow the same procedure as above. As shown in Fig. S5, distinct fluorescence recovery can be also observed with the introduction of miRNA-21 increasing from 10 pM to 800 pM. Nonetheless, the linear range and sensitivity are inferior to our synergistic quenching CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO system. Furtherly, our strategy was compared to diverse phosphors-based MB probes. As shown in Table S2, detection limit and detection range of our synergetic quenching MB probe are 1–2 orders of magnitudes lower and/or wider than that of other reported MB probes for miRNAs detection.

The selectively of this strategy was investigated by adding some interfering substance, such as base mismatched RNA sequences and other microRNAs such as miRNA-122, individually into this system instead of miRNA-21. As can be seen from Fig. 4 , when the concentration of target and interfering substances are 800 pM, only the addition of miRNA-21 makes the fluorescence intensity recovering significantly, while the introduction of base mismatch sequence and other RNA sequences do not change significantly, indicating that the system has good selectivity for miRNA-21.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of miRNA-21 in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Three individual human serum samples from tumor patients in different periods were first analyzed. Taking into consideration the linear range of our method and the normal miRNA-21 content in human serum, fresh samples were diluted 1000 times for miRNA-21 determination. As shown in Table 1 , miRNA-21 levels of sample 1–3 are calculated as 2.52 nM, 5.66 nM and 97.5 nM respectively, which are well consistent with that of qRT-PCR method (Er ≤ 15.3%). The relative high value of RSDs may be caused by stepwise dilution process and extremely low test concentration. Subsequently, sample 1 was selected as analytical medium to carry out standard addition experiment, and three various amounts of miRNA-21 (0.5, 40, 150 pM) were spiked. The recoveries range from 88.0% to 105% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) no more than 4.4% (Table S3), indicating the applicability of this CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO system in real samples for miRNA-21 quantification.

A MB probe that using green emitting carbon dots (CDs) as energy donor and organic dye and GO as synergistic quencher was reported for the detection of miRNA-21. Although the introduction of nanomaterials may increase the complexity of detection system and affect its stability to a certain extent, this effect is acceptable due to the small dosage of GO. More noteworthy is that the participation of GO can effectively increase the quenching efficiency, thereby improving the detection sensitivity. This CDs-MB-BHQ1/GO system can be used to detect very low level of miRNA-21 (as low as 500 fM) with satisfactory selectivity. The detection results of real serum samples is well consistent with that of qRT-PCR method. Moreover, this probe show great potential to develop into a universal detection platform through reasonable design of the MB sequence.

Mengyuan He: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Bo Zheng: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Software, Supervision, Writing – original draft. Ning Shang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation. Yu Xiao: Data curation, Investigation, Validation. Yuanhang Wei: Investigation, Visualization. Xuebo Hu: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804117), the Youth Fund Project of Xinyang Normal University (2022-QN-039), and the Nanhu Scholars Program for Young Scholars of Xinyang Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108593.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
